result:
A red leaf clings to the window, trembling in the night air like a lantern someone forgot to snuff. I lean closer, feel the cool pane transmit the hush from outside. The whole suburb is holding its breath; only the leaf refuses stillness, pulling against its invisible hinge. I wonder whether it finally lets go, will it circle my house three times like a farewell or cut directly to the ground and slide into the storm-drain darkness where all small things vanish.

Inside, the kettle begins its throat-clearing prelude—a metallic imitation of cicadas I won’t hear again until next July. Steam scribbles frantic runes on the glass, erasing both leaf and reflection. I pretend they constitute a message from my future self, though I can’t read mirror-writing without tilting my neck at a tragic angle. Perhaps gibberish is the proper dialect between ghosts and the living.

I turn off every lamp except the one above the stove, the color of weak tangerine. Books slump in tired piles; washing forms a soft city skyline across the couch. No one else is awake, so I conduct conversations with an imaginary orchestra—woodwinds alternating between memory’s minor thirds and anticipation’s brisk allegro. My heart beats too obviously, like a student clapping off tempo. Still, the ensemble is kind; it modulates whenever I speed or drag.

When the kettle whistles, it sounds oddly triumphant, as though water had learned to celebrate its own evaporation. I pour a cup, hold the heat against my palms, and transfer it to the spot where the leaf used to be.

message.content:
A red leaf clings to the window, trembling in the night air like a lantern someone forgot to snuff. I lean closer, feel the cool pane transmit the hush from outside. The whole suburb is holding its breath; only the leaf refuses stillness, pulling against its invisible hinge. I wonder whether it finally lets go, will it circle my house three times like a farewell or cut directly to the ground and slide into the storm-drain darkness where all small things vanish.

Inside, the kettle begins its throat-clearing prelude—a metallic imitation of cicadas I won’t hear again until next July. Steam scribbles frantic runes on the glass, erasing both leaf and reflection. I pretend they constitute a message from my future self, though I can’t read mirror-writing without tilting my neck at a tragic angle. Perhaps gibberish is the proper dialect between ghosts and the living.

I turn off every lamp except the one above the stove, the color of weak tangerine. Books slump in tired piles; washing forms a soft city skyline across the couch. No one else is awake, so I conduct conversations with an imaginary orchestra—woodwinds alternating between memory’s minor thirds and anticipation’s brisk allegro. My heart beats too obviously, like a student clapping off tempo. Still, the ensemble is kind; it modulates whenever I speed or drag.

When the kettle whistles, it sounds oddly triumphant, as though water had learned to celebrate its own evaporation. I pour a cup, hold the heat against my palms, and transfer it to the spot where the leaf used to be.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
